title: Identification and Validation of Distinct Latent Neurodevelopmental Profiles in the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development Study.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Regardless of the precise mechanism, all neurodevelopmental models of risk assume that, at the population level, there exist subgroups of individuals that share similar patterns of neural function and development-and that these subgroups somehow relate to psychiatric risk. However, the existence of multiple neurodevelopmental subgroups at the population level has not been assessed previously. METHODS: In the current study, cross-validated latent profile analysis was used to test for the presence of empirically derived, brain-based developmental subgroups using functional magnetic resonance imaging data from 6758 individuals (49.4% female; mean ageÂ = 9.94 years) in the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) study wave 1 release. Data were randomly split into training and testing samples. RESULTS: Analyses in the training sample (nÂ = 3379) identified a seven-profile solution (entropyÂ = 0.880) that was replicated in the held-out testing data (nÂ = 3379, entropyÂ = 0.890). Identified subgroups included a moderate group (66.8%), high reward (4.3%) and low reward (4.0%) groups, high inhibition (9.8%) and low inhibition (6.7%) groups, and high emotion regulation (4.0%) and low emotion regulation (4.3%) groups. Relative to the moderate group, other subgroups were characterized by more males (Ï2Â = 24.10, pÂ = .0005), higher proportions of individuals from lower-income households (Ï2Â = 122.17, p < .0001), poorer cognitive performance (ps < .0001), more screen time (FÂ = 6.80, p < .0001), heightened impulsivity (ps < .006), and higher rates of neurodevelopmental disorders (Ï2Â = 26.20, pÂ = .0002). CONCLUSIONS: These data demonstrate the existence of multiple, distinct neurodevelopmental subgroups at the population level. They indicate that these empirically derived, brain-based developmental profiles relate to differences in clinical features, even at a young age, and prior to the peak period of risk for the development of psychopathology.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Paper reports screen time as a comparative variable but does NOT use qualifying smartphone/SM exposure as primary variable. Screen time is measured and compared by demographic profiles, but there is no separate measurement of smartphone ownership, app use, texting, SM platforms, or qualifying scales (MPIQ, PSMUS, SMAQ, SNS-A, Bergen). Per B5, general screen time without device/platform specificity does not meet FT-IC2. Measurement is latent profile analysis of fMRI brain activation in reward, inhibition, and emotion regulation domains.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Screen time measured; abstract states 'more screen time' as comparative finding across neurodevelopmental profiles (B5 qualifier: screen time is qualifying variable; applies B1)

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: No SM ↔ non-SM inferential tests. The paper is a latent profile analysis (LPA) of brain activation patterns. Screen time is included as a covariate in demographic comparisons (ANOVA, χ² tests) but is not the primary exposure in any analysis testing associations with behavioral or clinical outcomes. Brain activation is the DV; demographics and screen time are characteristics of profiles.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Latent profile analysis with inferential comparisons; chi-square tests and F-tests comparing profiles on screen time (χ²=6.80, p<.0001)

title: Current, future and potential use of mobile and wearable technologies and social media data in the ABCD study to increase understanding of contributors to child health.
abstract: Mobile and wearable technologies and novel methods of data collection are innovating health-related research. These technologies and methods allow for multi-system level capture of data across environmental, physiological, behavioral, and psychological domains. In the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, there is great potential for harnessing the acceptability, accessibility, and functionality of mobile and social technologies for in-vivo data capture to precisely measure factors, and interactions between factors, that contribute to childhood and adolescent neurodevelopment and psychosocial and health outcomes. Here we discuss advances in mobile and wearable technologies and methods of analysis of geospatial, ecologic, social network and behavioral data. Incorporating these technologies into the ABCD study will allow for interdisciplinary research on the effects of place, social interactions, environment, and substance use on health and developmental outcomes in children and adolescents.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Resource/technology review paper, not empirical measurement paper. Discusses mobile and wearable technologies and social media data but does NOT report any primary measurements or uses of qualifying SM/smartphone variables in original ABCD data analysis. Paper outlines 'current, future and potential use' of technologies; no extractable SM exposure variables from ABCD data analyzed here. Per FT-IC5 binding (B14), resource/data papers are excluded as NOT empirical analysis.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Mobile phone use, smartphone technologies, wearable devices (accelerometers, Fitbit), social media data, and passive sensing methods discussed as current/future data collection approaches in ABCD.

title: Youth screen use in the ABCDÂ® study.
abstract: Adolescent screen usage is ubiquitous and influences development and behavior. Longitudinal screen usage data coupled with psychometrically valid constructs of problematic behaviors can provide insights into these relationships. We describe methods by which the screen usage questionnaire was developed in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, demonstrate longitudinal changes in screen usage via child report and describe data harmonization baseline-year 2. We further include psychometric analyses of adapted social media and video game addiction scales completed by youth. Nearly 12,000 children ages 9-10 years at baseline and their parents were included in the analyses. The social media addiction questionnaire (SMAQ) showed similar factor structure and item loadings across sex and race/ethnicities, but that item intercepts varied across both sex and race/ethnicity. The videogame addiction questionnaire (VGAQ) demonstrated the same configural, metric and scalar invariance across racial and ethnic groups, however differed across sex. Video gaming and online social activity increased over ages 9/10-11/12 (p'sÂ <Â 0.001). Compared with boys, girls engaged in greater social media use (pÂ <Â .001) and demonstrated higher ratings on the SMAQ (pÂ <Â .001). Compared with girls, boys played more video games (pÂ <Â .001) and demonstrated higher ratings on the VGAQ (pÂ <Â .001). Time spent playing video games increased more steeply for boys than girls from age 9/10-11/12 years (pÂ <Â .001). Black youth demonstrated significantly higher SMAQ and VGAQ scores compared to all other racial/ethnic groups. These data show the importance of considering different screen modalities beyond total screen use and point towards clear demographic differences in use patterns. With these comprehensive data, ABCD is poised to address critical questions about screen usage changes across adolescence.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Social media (SMAQ—6-item Bergen Social Media Addiction Scale adapted Year 2); Video gaming (VGAQ—8-item Bergen Video Game Addiction Questionnaire Year 2); time on social media; online social activity; video gaming time. Qualifies under B1 (YSTS with separate SM/gaming coefficients) and B6 (gaming + SM with separate coefficients)
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: B12: Pure measurement paper with psychometric analyses (SMAQ/VGAQ factor structure, invariance testing across demographics). Descriptive statistics only (means, comparisons by sex/race). No SM or smartphone as IV with coefficient predicting external outcome.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: T-tests comparing sex differences (p < .001 for girls higher SMAQ, boys higher VGAQ); chi-squared tests; ANOVA; CFA/factor analysis (χ2 tests for invariance across sex/race); linear mixed-effects models; descriptive statistics with p-values
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: B12: Only SM-vs-SM concordance (factor invariance testing, SMAQ/VGAQ equivalence across groups). No inferential test of SM ↔ non-SM-outcome relationship.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Tables 1, 4, 5 report χ2 values, p-values, RMSEA, CFI, SRMR; Figures 1–3 show proportions with 95% CIs; t-values and p-values reported throughout (p < .001 for multiple comparisons)
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: No extractable numeric SM ↔ outcome coefficient. Chi-square and p-values reported for factor structure comparisons, but these test measurement invariance, not SM predicting behavioral/health outcomes.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD study. Measurement paper demonstrating screen usage questionnaire development, longitudinal changes baseline–Year 2, and psychometric analyses of SMAQ/VGAQ
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: B13: Pure measurement/concordance paper. Adapted SMAQ and VGAQ scales, demonstrated psychometric properties across populations. No original SM → outcome analysis. Not eligible under B13 (would require at least one SM ↔ non-SM coefficient).

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Smartphone/SM is the DEPENDENT variable in all analyses: time on SM, SMAQ, video gaming time, VGAQ are outcomes. Predictors are exclusively demographics: sex, race/ethnicity, age (per Table 1, Methods). All analyses are longitudinal/prospective (baseline to Year 1 to Year 2). Paper has NO SM↔non-SM outcome analysis. Satisfies FT-EC7 binding conditions (a), (b), (c) with no cross-sectional demographic-SM carve-out.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Paper fails FT_IC2 and FT_IC5 (measurement-only); EC7 logic does not apply.
